era_context: The early 12th century was characterised by the consolidation of the Crusader States in the Levant following the First Crusade (1096–1099), the reign of Henry I in England and Normandy (beginning in 1100), the Song Dynasty's continued dominance in China, and the fragmentation of the Seljuk Empire into regional sultanates. This was a period of significant military activity, administrative development, and cross-cultural contact.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)

events.title: Crusader defeat at the Horns of Hattin (alternative dating; primary event in 1187)
events.description: This entry requires correction: the Battle of Hattin occurred in 1187, not 1102. In 1102, Crusader forces were engaged in ongoing military campaigns to consolidate their control of the Levant. A significant Crusader military engagement occurred in 1102 at the Battle of Ramla, where Crusader forces under Baldwin I defeated a Muslim relief force. This victory secured Crusader control of the region and demonstrated their military superiority in pitched battles.
key_figures: Baldwin I of Jerusalem (King of Jerusalem)

sources.name: Fulcher of Chartres, History of the Expedition to Jerusalem

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ramla in 1102 is well-documented in Crusade sources. The date is consistently given as 1102 in near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: 1099-001 (First Crusade and establishment of Crusader States)

events.title: Henry I of England consolidates power; conflict with Robert Curthose continues
events.description: Henry I of England, having secured his position through his accession in 1100, continued to consolidate his power and strengthen his authority. In 1102, he was engaged in ongoing conflicts with his brother Robert Curthose, Duke of Normandy, over control of Normandy and the English throne. These conflicts would eventually lead to the decisive Battle of Tinchebrai in 1106. In 1102, Henry was also implementing administrative reforms and strengthening the royal court.
key_figures: Henry I of England (King of England and Duke of Normandy)
Robert Curthose (Duke of Normandy, Henry's brother)
events.certainty_note: Henry I's consolidation of power and conflicts with Robert Curthose are well-documented in general terms. Specific events in 1102 are less distinctly recorded than the broader pattern of his governance.

events.title: Bohemond I of Antioch remains imprisoned by the Danishmends
events.description: Bohemond I, Prince of Antioch, remained in captivity with the Danishmend Turks, having been captured in 1100. During his imprisonment, his nephew Tancred served as regent of Antioch, managing the principality's affairs and conducting military campaigns. Bohemond's continued captivity affected the political dynamics of the Crusader States and demonstrated the ongoing military threat posed by Turkish forces in Anatolia. He would be released in 1103.
events.certainty_note: Bohemond's imprisonment is well-documented. He was captured in 1100 and released in 1103, so his continued captivity in 1102 is confirmed.

events.title: Song Dynasty continues administrative consolidation under Emperor Huizong
events.description: Emperor Huizong of the Song Dynasty (r. 1100–1125) continued his reign, which was marked by cultural patronage, artistic achievement, and administrative reforms. In 1102, the Song court was engaged in managing the complex bureaucratic apparatus of the empire, dealing with military pressures from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty to the north, and maintaining the sophisticated examination system that characterised Song governance. Huizong's reign was notable for its cultural flourishing but also for increasing military vulnerability.
key_figures: Huizong of Song (Emperor of the Song Dynasty)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Huizong's reign and administrative activities are well-documented in Chinese sources. Specific events in 1102 are less distinctly recorded, but the general context of his reign is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Hattin, which is one of the most significant Crusader defeats, occurred in 1187, not 1102. This is a major chronological error that required correction. The Battle of Ramla in 1102 is a Crusader victory, not a defeat. The significance of 1102 as a distinct year in English history is less pronounced than 1106 (Tinchebrai) or 1100 (Henry I's accession). The military pressure on the Song Dynasty from the Jurchen Jin Dynasty is well-documented, but the specific events of 1102 are not distinctly recorded.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1102 is dominated by Western sources, particularly Crusade chronicles and English administrative records. Islamic and Byzantine perspectives on events in the Levant exist but are less frequently consulted in English-language historiography. Chinese sources provide detailed administrative records but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The year 1102 appears to be a relatively significant year for Crusader military consolidation (with the Battle of Ramla) but less significant for other regions. The bias toward military and political events over economic or social history is pronounced in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Ramla in 1102 was part of the ongoing consolidation of the Crusader States established in 1099
Henry I's consolidation efforts in 1102 were part of his broader strategy following his accession in 1100
Henry I's conflicts with Robert Curthose in 1102 culminated in the decisive victory at Tinchebrai in 1106
Bohemond's continued captivity in 1102 was a consequence of his capture in 1100
